TOP 2015: Focused on improving transparency in journal publications through structured standards meant to support gradual, systemic change.
TOP 2025: Centers on promoting the verifiability of empirical research claims, and is designed to be used by a wider range of organizations (journals, funders, institutions, and societies).

TOP 2015: 8 separate standards: Citation, Data Transparency, Analytic Code Transparency, Materials Transparency, Design & Analysis Transparency, Preregistration of Studies, Preregistration of Analysis Plans, Replication.
TOP 2025: Organized into three clearer categories: Research Practices (7): study registration, study protocols, analysis plans, reporting, materials, data, code; Verification Practices (2): results transparency, computational reproducibility; Verification Studies (4): replication, registered reports, multiverse, many-analyst.

TOP 2015: Level 0: Not addressed or Adopted; Level 1: Disclosure; Level 2: Requirement; Level 3: Verification.
TOP 2025: Level 1: Disclosed; Level 2: Shared & Cited; Level 3: Certified (third-party confirmation or independent verification).

TOP 2015: Treated as its own standalone standard (citation of data, materials, and code).
TOP 2025: Incorporated directly into Level 2 ("Shared & Cited") across all Research Practices to create consistency.

TOP 2015: Two standards: preregistration of studies and preregistration of analysis plans. These were often interpreted together.
TOP 2025: Broader, clearer language: study registration, study protocols, and analysis plans are now separate standards with clarified timing requirements.

TOP 2015: Included as the "Design and Analysis Transparency" standard.
TOP 2025: Renamed "Reporting Transparency", emphasizing the use of reporting guidelines (e.g., EQUATOR Network).

TOP 2015: Implied verification appears only at Level 3 within each standard (e.g., independent reproducibility checks).
TOP 2025: Split out more explicitly: Certification of sharing practices (Research Practices, Level 3) and Verification Practices for results transparency and computational reproducibility.

TOP 2015: Replication was one of the eight standards; Registered Reports were treated as Level 3 within this standard.
TOP 2025: Expanded into a broader category of Verification Studies: replication, Registered Reports (now separate), plus multiverse and many-analyst studies.
Intended for journals and funders, preprint servers, universities, professional societies, and training programs.

TOP 2015: Encouraged modular adoption. So, journals choose which standards and levels to implement.
TOP 2025: Maintains flexibility but encourages more coordinated adoption across organizations to support broader, systemic change.
